title: CSE 101 — Algorithms (UCSC, Winter 2020)
description: Interactive visualizations of the data structures and algorithms from Patricia Lopez's CSE 101. All three demos run the original course C++ unmodified — N-Queens uses hw2/NQueens/Board.cpp + Queens.cpp; AVL Wordrange uses hw3/Wordrange/AVL.cpp; Six-Degrees BFS uses hw4/Sixdegrees/graph.cpp — compiled to WebAssembly with thin step-by-step iterator shims. The JavaScript layer only handles rendering; the algorithms themselves are exactly what was submitted for grading in 2020.

assets.title: HW2 — N-Queens (real C++ → WASM)
assets.description: Place N queens on an N×N board such that no two attack each other. The Board / Queens classes from the original CSE 101 homework are compiled to WebAssembly — the only thing JavaScript adds is a step-driver and the canvas renderer. Watch the backtracker place queens column-by-column with each conflict and unwinding step animated.

assets.title: HW3 — AVL Wordrange (real C++ → WASM)
assets.description: Self-balancing BST of strings. The AVL.cpp from the original course is compiled to WebAssembly; the JavaScript layer only renders the tree. Insert words, run range queries (count of keys lexicographically between Lo and Hi), and watch the tree balance itself with each operation — all using the algorithm as it was submitted for grading.

assets.title: HW4 — Six-Degrees BFS (real C++ → WASM)
assets.description: Find the shortest path between any two actors in a movie graph using BFS. The graph.cpp from the original course is compiled to WebAssembly; JavaScript handles only the input form and rendering. Bundled with a small synthetic movie list (~40 films, ~70 actors); paste your own edge list to explore custom graphs. The original course used a 10MB IMDb-derived dataset that we deliberately do NOT ship — the algorithm is the interesting part.
